s1_b1: Maha has a block of ice, a small heater, and a timer. The block must be fully water exactly when the timer ends.
s1_b2: One goal. Not melted early. Not still icy at the end. Done, right on the buzzer.
s1_b3: She turns the heater high. The thermometer races up, then stalls dead at zero while the ice just sits there.
s1_b4: She thinks the heater died. She turns it higher. The needle still won't move off zero.
s1_b5: Then, all at once, the ice is gone and the temperature shoots up — and overshoots, boiling before the buzzer.
s1_b6: She swaps to a metal cup to help. It heats the water faster than she expects and ruins her timing again.
s1_b7: She wasn't short of heat. She was short of a budget — energy has more than one bill to pay here.
s1_b8: Some energy raises temperature — and how much it takes depends on the substance and how much there is.
s1_b9: And a huge chunk pays only to change state — melting the ice — with the thermometer flat the entire time.
s1_b10: And the rate the bill arrives depends on what carries the heat — fast through metal, slow otherwise.
s1_b11: Hidden melt cost, temperature cost, delivery rate. Miss one line and the timing is always wrong.
s1_b12: Before she touches the heater again — she builds it where she can see every line of the budget.
s1_b13: Energy Forms and Changes. She sets the heat rate and the amount. Turns the energy chunks and the thermometer on.
s1_b14: Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before she does.
s1_b15: What heat rate makes the temperature cost plus the hidden melt cost finish exactly when the timer hits zero?
s1_b16: Then she presses play. And she watches the energy chunks and the flat stretch, not the ice.
s1_b17: After — she explains the gap. Finished early? She underpaid the hidden melt. Late? She overpaid the rest.
s1_b18: She adjusts the heat rate. Predicts again. Three tries. That's all she gets.
s1_b19: Her first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss exposes a line she forgot.
s1_b20: The melt-on-time was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — where did the energy go while the thermometer stood still?
s1_b21: And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the tiny match and the warm tub — hotter is not the same as more heat?
s1_b22: And the water that drank energy and barely warmed, while the oil shot up — the same heat, different substance?
s1_b23: And the spoon, the churning pot, the Sun across empty space — three ways heat ever arrives? And the thermometer that stuck at zero while the ice quietly drank it all?
s1_b24: This melt was all four at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.
